title: Fluid Management in a NYI (Chapter 12)
learningObjectives: Describe how maintenance fluids (feeds) and maintenance fluids (intravenous) are calculated according to weight and age.
Describe how to monitor a NYI who is receiving IV fluids.
Describe when and how to introduce oral fluids (feeds).

subsections.title: Exclusive Breastfeeding & General Feeding Principles
subsections.contentSummary: Breastfeeding is the preferred method for most newborn infants (NYI).  If breastfeeding on demand is impossible, expressed breast milk is given.  Maintenance fluid needs replace losses and are calculated on the infant’s age and weight.  Early feeds are small and increased gradually; feeds are given every 2‑3 hours via naso‑/oro‑gastric tube or cup depending on maturity.

subsections.title: Calculation of Feeds for NYI (breast milk)
subsections.contentSummary: Table‑based daily fluid requirements: Day 1 = 60 ml/kg/day, Day 2 = 90 ml/kg/day, Day 3 = 120 ml/kg/day, Day 4 = 150 ml/kg/day, Day 7 & low‑birth‑weight infants can be increased to 180 ml/kg/day once oral feeds are tolerated.

header: How to calculate maintenance fluid requirements
rows: Increase to 180 ml/kg per day after a few days if oral feeds are tolerated

subsections.title: Feeding of Premature Infants
subsections.contentSummary: Infants < 34 weeks gestation may not be able to breastfeed; they receive tube or cup feeds, given in smaller volumes more frequently (ideally every 2 hours, sometimes hourly).  Reference wall‑charts provide volume calculations based on age, maturity and feeding frequency.

subsections.title: Indications / Contra‑indications for Oral Feeding
subsections.contentSummary: IV fluids are indicated when oral feeding is contraindicated (medical or surgical).  Medical reasons include severe/recurrent apnoea, severe respiratory distress, frequent convulsions, unconsciousness.  Surgical reasons include bowel obstruction, necrotising enterocolitis, abdominal wall defects (relative), tracheo‑oesophageal fistula.

rows:
Severe or recurrent apnoea
Severe respiratory distress
Frequent convulsions
Unconsciousness

Bowel obstruction
Necrotising enterocolitis
Abdominal wall defects (relative)
Tracheo‑oesophageal fistula

subsections.title: Which fluid?
subsections.contentSummary: Maintenance fluid replaces losses and is calculated from age and weight; IV fluids should not exceed 100 ml/kg/day to avoid over‑hydration.  Wall‑charts give hourly rates based on weight/age.  When a syringe pump is unavailable, drip rate is calculated using drops per ml of the giving set (20, 15 or 60 drops/ml).

header: How many drops in one ml of fluid?
rows:
1 ml = 20 drops (most common)
1 ml = 15 drops (for blood)
1 ml = 60 drops (paediatric)

Amount of fluid (ml) × drops/ml ÷ time (minutes) = drops/minute
Example: 40 ml/hr with 20‑drop set → (40 × 20)/60 = 13 drops/min

subsections.title: Monitor the IV infusion very carefully
subsections.contentSummary: Use a monitoring sheet, calculate and check drip rate and infused volume each hour.  Clinical monitoring includes heart rate, pulse volume, respiratory rate, skin perfusion, oedema/puffy eyes, daily weight, urine output, and inspection of cannula site.  Weight and urine output are the best overall guides to fluid adequacy.  Oral feeds are introduced when safe, IV rates are reduced accordingly, and IV fluids are discontinued once oral intake reaches ≈ 2/3 of total requirements.

subsections.title: Introducing enteral feeds when the baby has been on IV fluids
subsections.contentSummary: If the infant is stable and there is no contraindication, start enteral feeds at 10 ml/kg/day, check residual volume before next feed, and increase slowly while weaning IV fluids.  If residual > 25 % or abdominal distension, do not increase volume.  Vomiting warrants restarting IV fluids and stopping enteral feeds.  If a contraindication exists, continue IV fluids and restrict fluids if signs of overload appear (weight gain, tachycardia, fast breathing, oedema, puffy eyes, excessive urine output).

header: If stable and feeds are not contraindicated
If there is a contraindication to oral fluids/feeds
rows:
Start enteral feeds 10 ml/kg/day
Measure residual volume before next feed
If residual > 25 % or abdominal distension → do not increase volume
If tolerated → slowly increase feeds and wean IV fluids when oral intake ≈ 2/3 maintenance
If vomiting → restart IV fluids, stop enteral feeds

Continue IV fluids according to age
Do not increase fluids; consider restricting if:
- Weight gain
- Tachycardia
- Fast breathing
- Oedema
- Puffy eyes
- Excessive urine output

sections.title: E 10 Use of the Syringe Driver (Pump) – Kasupe

subsections.title: Principles of Using a Syringe Driver
subsections.contentSummary: A brief overview (link to detailed page) of the principles for using a syringe driver/pump for IV fluid administration.
